title: Are We in a Defense Tech Bubble?
gist: BCG and Vertical Research Partners argue that despite billions in private capital flowing to affordable-mass and expendable defense platforms (drones, loitering munitions), exquisite legacy systems will keep dominating defense-aviation profit pools through 2033 because newer platforms grow off tiny bases, lack durable sustainment revenue, and shift R&D risk onto self-funded new entrants. Incumbent primes are advised to treat these markets as a ring-fenced portfolio supplement rather than a core-business pivot.

insights.claim: In 2025, exquisite defense-aviation systems represented about $65 billion of US and EU spend, versus roughly $5 billion for affordable-mass systems and just $55 million for expendable systems, so legacy primes still control the overwhelming bulk of the market.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: The addressable pool for defense-tech upstarts is still tiny in absolute dollars, so headline growth rates should not be mistaken for near-term revenue scale.
insights.quote: Exquisite systems comprised about $65 billion in the US and EU in 2025, compared with roughly $5 billion for affordable mass systems and just $55 million for expendable systems.
insights.locator: Legacy players will continue to claim the bulk

insights.claim: Even though expendable systems are projected to grow at 35% to 40% CAGR and affordable mass at 15% to 20% versus just 2% to 3% for exquisite systems, exquisite platforms will still make up more than 80% of the defense-aviation market by 2033 because the newer categories start from far smaller bases.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Investors should discount the disruption narrative: high CAGRs off small bases leave incumbents structurally dominant for the next decade.
insights.quote: even with that disparity in growth rates, exquisite systems will still comprise more than 80% of the market by 2033, because the newer categories are growing from smaller bases

insights.claim: The US Department of War's Other Transaction Authority (OTA) program, which lets the government contract with non-traditional companies for research, prototypes, and production, grew from $1.8 billion in 2016 to more than $18 billion in 2025.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: A tenfold rise in non-traditional acquisition dollars signals a durable procurement channel that lowers entry barriers for defense-tech startups and warrants monitoring.
insights.quote: The program has increased from $1.8 billion in 2016 to more than $18 billion in 2025.
insights.locator: The Rapid Rise of Nontraditional Competitors

insights.claim: Anduril and General Atomics were selected to build prototypes for the US Air Force's Collaborative Combat Aircraft, an uncrewed vehicle flying alongside crewed jets, beating incumbent primes Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and Boeing.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: A concrete case of non-traditional players displacing primes on a marquee autonomy program signals genuine competitive risk to incumbent core franchises.
insights.quote: Anduril and General Atomics were both selected to develop prototypes, beating out prime contractors Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, and Boeing.

insights.claim: There is a real risk to incumbents' core business if affordable-mass players can adapt the production systems they built for speed and apply them to lower-rate production programs traditionally owned by primes.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: The moat around primes may erode from the bottom up, so watch whether upstarts move up the value chain into higher-rate programs.
insights.quote: There is a real question as to whether the core business of incumbents is at risk, particularly if affordable mass players can adapt the production system they have built for speed and apply it to lower-rate production programs.

insights.claim: Most manufacturers of affordable-mass and expendable defense systems are not yet profitable despite huge demand for their products, leaving prime contractors with a capital-allocation dilemma between stable exquisite programs and higher-growth but unproven segments.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Unprofitable demand-rich startups may offer attractive M&A entry points for primes, or value traps for the private capital already invested.
insights.quote: Most manufacturers of affordable mass and expendable systems are not yet profitable, despite seeing huge demand for their products.
insights.locator: Yet while these new solutions are succeeding

insights.claim: Exquisite platforms typically derive about half of their lifetime profit from sustainment, with the F/A-18E/F Super Hornet generating over $10 billion of sustainment profit across a $20.3 billion lifetime program, whereas expendable systems generate nearly all their value at initial production and purchase.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: The recurring, high-margin sustainment tail is the real prize in legacy programs and is largely absent from expendable business models, reshaping how to value each.
insights.quote: Exquisite platforms typically derive about half of their lifetime profit from sustainment (over $10 billion for the Super Hornet across a $20.3 billion lifetime program).

insights.claim: US defense budgets fell roughly 37% after the Cold War and 30% to 45% in the drawdowns after Iraq and Afghanistan, and this contraction risk falls harder on companies that earn most profit from volume sales rather than sustainment.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Volume-dependent defense-tech names are more exposed to peacetime budget drawdowns than sustainment-heavy primes, a key downside-scenario consideration.
insights.quote: In the US, for example, defense budgets fell roughly 37% after the Cold War and 30% to 45% in the drawdowns following operations in Iraq and Afghanistan.
insights.locator: The durability of profits from sustainment

insights.claim: Primes retain customer relationships even in peacetime through sustainment and training needs and permanent posts at customer bases, making their revenue far less volatile and less reliant on growing defense budgets than new-tech players who lack any similar precedent.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Embedded customer intimacy is a structural moat for primes that discounts their revenue volatility relative to upstarts dependent on wartime demand.
insights.quote: primes' revenue in this area is far less volatile and reliant on growing defense budgets

insights.claim: In exquisite programs, Tier 1 suppliers of major components like engines, mission and communication systems, and sensors can capture 40% to 50% of lifecycle profits independently of the platform prime.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Much of the profit in legacy programs sits with component suppliers, so investors should look beyond platform primes to Tier 1 franchises for value.
insights.quote: Tier 1 suppliers—particularly suppliers of major components like engines, mission and communication systems, and sensors—can capture 40% to 50% of lifecycle profits independently of the platform prime

insights.claim: Expendable defense programs have shallow supply chains with value concentrated in owning the platform's intellectual property and being its sole production source, but most sellers lack the qualified suppliers, compliance, and certification experience needed to win larger and more complex contracts.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: IP ownership is the upstarts' edge, but a certification and qualification gap caps their ability to scale up-market, a bottleneck to watch.
insights.quote: Expendable programs have shallower supply chains, with value focused on owning the intellectual property behind a given platform and being the sole source of its production.

insights.claim: Traditional defense programs often use government-funded development and negotiated fee structures that cap upside but reduce primes' capital at risk, whereas affordable-mass and expendable contractors largely self-fund R&D with venture or private capital and bear the bulk of the financial risk.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: The R&D risk-reward has flipped onto self-funded new entrants: winners own the IP and become sole-source, losers face existential risk, sharpening the binary nature of these bets.
insights.quote: affordable mass and expendable contractors largely fund their own R&D, often with the aid of venture or private capital, and thus bear the bulk of the financial risk
insights.locator: R&D risks shift to new entrants

insights.claim: Europe's push toward lower-cost defense tech is constrained by procurement fragmented across national ministries of defense and a focus on rebuilding basic defense capabilities before committing to newer technologies, even as many privately funded startups target the market.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: European defense-tech adoption will lag the US due to fragmented buyers and capability-rebuild priorities, tempering near-term revenue expectations there.
insights.quote: the procurement is more fragmented among ministries of defense and countries are focused on rebuilding basic defense capabilities before committing to newer types of defense technologies
insights.locator: Europe is moving in the same direction

insights.claim: Primes entering affordable-mass and expendable markets should ring-fence those businesses with their own P&L and a structurally separate operating model, so large players with heavy overhead can compete on price against smaller rivals while the core business keeps its legacy model.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Watch whether primes create separate P&L units for defense-tech; failing to segregate overhead structures will leave them uncompetitive on price.
insights.quote: Ring-fence affordable mass and expendable businesses with their own P&L. That will help large players with significant overhead compete on price with smaller competitors.

insights.claim: The recommended path for primes is a portfolio approach that treats affordable-mass and expendable markets as a supplement to the core rather than a replacement, using ecosystem partnerships, minority investments, and acquisitions to access innovation faster than building it internally.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: Expect primes to pursue minority stakes and M&A in defense-tech rather than organic pivots, framing likely deal flow and exit opportunities for private capital.
insights.quote: Use partnerships, minority investments, and acquisitions to access innovation more quickly—without committing the time and organizational resources to develop it internally.
insights.locator: The Value of a Portfolio Approach

insights.claim: BCG concludes exquisite platforms will likely remain the dominant source of defense-industry profits for the foreseeable future, and winners will be those who understand where long-duration aviation economics dominate versus where faster-cycle autonomy economics are emerging.
insights.speaker: https://www.bcg.com/about/people/experts/diana-dimitrova
insights.so_what: The central verdict answers the title's bubble question: no imminent regime change, so position for coexistence of legacy and autonomy economics rather than wholesale disruption.
insights.quote: Exquisite platforms will likely remain the dominant source of profits in the defense industry for the foreseeable future, but incumbents should understand the threat that comes from newer competitors and less-expensive solutions.
